data.name: Vozinha
data.description: One of the living legends of Cape Verdean football and, along with Ryan Mendes, the only player to play at every major tournament Cape Verde have featured in, Josimar "Vozinha" Dias is a national treasure. Arguably the country’s most beloved footballer, he only left the country at 25, turning professional when he signed for the Angolan side Progresso do Sambizanga. Six months later he made his Cape Verde debut in a 2-0 win against Cameroon that helped to send the country to their first ever Africa Cup of Nations in 2013. “The first game he played I was scared because I didn't know what he was capable of,” Fernando Neves, Vozinha’s captain and centre-back on the day, told the Guardian. “But after 10 minutes I wasn't scared. For me he's the best goalkeeper in Cape Verde's history.” Now Vozinha will make his World Cup bow a week after turning 40.

data.name: Stopira
data.description: Praia’s favourite son, Ianique dos Santos Tavares was born and raised in the Achada de Santo António neighbourhood of the nation’s capital. After breaking through at Sporting Praia, he moved to Portugal before spending a decade with Fehervar in Hungary, where he now has citizenship. Named after the French footballer Yannick Stopyra, the red-haired right-back has been with the Blue Sharks for nearly two decades. He retired from international football in June 2024 but was convinced to rejoin the national team for the World Cup qualifying campaign. “Even if he doesn’t play, Stopira is one of the most important players in this team,” the technical director, Rui Costa, told the Guardian. Stopira underlined that importance when he scored the third goal in Cape Verde’s 3-0 win over Eswatini to seal qualification in his hometown.

data.name: Diney
data.description: Despite spending more than a decade in Portugal, at the last World Cup Borges had to cheer against the Lusos, instead supporting his adopted nation, Morocco. The aggressive, battling centre-back was enjoying an unexpected spell with the Moroccan club Asfar Rabat where he enjoyed four successful years. Borges has been with Cape Verde for nine years but has only got a proper run in the team in the past 12 months. An anterior cruciate injury to the star defender Logan Costa thrust Borges into the fray and he took his chance. He struck up a solid partnership with Pico Lopes and only Dailon Livramento scored more goals in qualifying for the Blue Sharks. If Costa returns to start, Borges will consider himself a very unlucky man.

data.name: Pico Lopes
data.description: Surely a lot of Irish people will be supporting Cape Verde at the World Cup, thanks to the boy from Crumlin, via São Nicolau. Roberto "Pico" Lopes was born and raised in Dublin but was called up to the Blue Sharks for the first time via LinkedIn. “I didn’t understand. I just thought it was a welcome message … so I ignored it,” Pico told CNN in 2024. The Shamrock Rovers defender finally put the Portuguese message through Google Translate nine months later and has not looked back after making his international debut in 2019. The shirt-tucked-in, no-nonsense centre-back has become a mainstay at the heart of the Cape Verde defence ever since, playing in the 2021 and 2023 Africa Cup of Nations. It’s a remarkable rise for the 34-year-old who a decade ago was working as a mortgage adviser while playing football part-time. Despite not speaking any Portuguese and slowly learning Kriol, Pico is a fan favourite and, as one of the federation’s vice-presidents told the Guardian, “a future captain” of the Blue Sharks.

data.name: Logan Costa
data.description: In a team full of talented journeymen, Logan Costa is perhaps Cape Verde’s sole elite player. The French-born centre-back (a cousin to Dailon Livramento) broke through at Toulouse, where he scored twice in the final of the club's historic Coupe de France win before moving to Villarreal. The powerful ball-playing defender is the only Cape Verdean playing in one of Europe’s top five leagues and is the nation’s most important player, having formed a solid partnership with Pico Lopes. “He has all the necessary ingredients to become even stronger than he already is,” the former Blue Shark Georges Santos, now a scout at West Ham, says. “Villarreal are a very good team, but I could see him play for Barça, Real Madrid and Atlético as he is a quick and intelligent player and can defend.” Suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury during pre-season, returning to the pitch only in Villarreal’s penultimate game of the season. His fitness will likely define whether the Blue Sharks fail or succeed in North America.

data.name: Kevin Pina
data.description: Born and raised in Praia, Pina thought any hopes of a football career were over when he emigrated to America. But a chance encounter with the former Blue Sharks captain Carlos "Caló" Morais changed everything. “I saw him playing in a street game in Brocton and went to his father to convince him to come back to Cape Verde,” Caló told the Guardian. After six months, Pina failed trials at Casa Pia and Benfica before signing with Chaves. He’s now a Russian league champion with Krasnodar and the midfield engine for Cape Verde. “I still have the letter from Benfica saying he's not good enough,” laughs Caló.

data.name: Jovane Cabral
data.description: Born on Santiago island, Cabral moved to Portugal when he was 14 to join Sporting’s academy, where he spent a decade. He was a key cog in Ruben Amorim’s side that ended the club’s 19-year title drought, with the former Manchester United coach describing him as “perhaps the most important player when we first arrived”. His success in Portugal prompted him to turn down a call-up to Cape Verde (having featured only in one friendly), instead wanting to try to naturalise as Portuguese and play with Cristiano Ronaldo et al. A series of injuries and subsequent loss of form derailed both his club and international career. The winger eventually returned to the Blue Sharks and heads into the World Cup without a club, having ended his time with Estrela Amadora.

data.name: João Paulo Fernandes
data.description: He was born in Cidade Velha on Santiago island, and Fernandes's story is a classic example of the challenges of becoming a professional footballer in Cape Verde: from his electrician father sacrificing hours everyday to ferry him to Praia for training as a kid to failing to move to Portugal initially because of a work-permit problem. The left-back who can play in midfield now plays in Romania with FCSB. So popular is João Paulo with the club that when he initially struggled to get a work permit, Gigi Becali, the club owner and a former member of the European Parliament, said: “I’ll just have to intervene, as a parliamentarian, to write an official letter for his work permit."

data.name: Gilson Benchimol
data.description: Born in Praia, Tavares moved to Portugal as a child, playing for various local clubs in Lisbon as a right-back. It wasn’t until he was signed by Estoril that he was moved up front to play as a forward as a 19-year-old. A strong start to life in Estoril led to links to clubs around Europe, including Nice, Monaco, Milan and Juventus. Eventually he signed for Benfica on a loan before making the unexpected move to the Russian side Akron Tolyatti in 2024. Despite the left-field career choice, Benchimol has been a mainstay of Bubista’s side. In March 2022 he became the first and only player to score a hat-trick for the Blue Sharks when he put three past Liechtenstein in a friendly.

data.name: Jamiro Monteiro
data.description: The creative heartbeat of Cape Verde, Jamiro Monteiro is another of the national-team players who is returning “home” to the US having spent five successful seasons split between Philadelphia Union and San Jose Earthquakes. Capable of playing deeper in midfield or as a No 10, Monteiro is combative and a solid ball carrier, but he credits his development as an attacking threat to the man who gave him his professional breakthrough at the Dutch club Cambuur, Arne Slot. “He saw that winning balls back was one of my strengths, and I combined that with playing more forward. That’s how I’ve grown as a player,” he told Voetbal International.

data.name: Garry Rodrigues
data.description: There are more players born in Rotterdam than any other city in the Cape Verde squad and Rodrigues is the elder statesman of the group, becoming the first Dutch-born player to represent the Blue Sharks in 2013. But for the mercurial winger, there was only one nation he ever wanted to represent. “My father was always there for me with my football and it was a dream for him that I would play for the Cape Verdean team,” he told FourFourTwo in 2024. Despite coming through Feyenoord’s prestigious Varkenoord academy he failed to secure a professional contract for almost four years after being released. He played amateur football in the Netherlands while working night shifts in the Dutch postal service. Sixteen years on from that, he has enjoyed a career that has taken him to clubs such as Galatasaray, Fenerbahce and Olympiakos while amassing more than 60 caps for Cape Verde.

data.name: Mércio Rosa
data.description: The victim of Vozinha’s success and longevity. Despite being a solid goalkeeper ever since he starred in an under-19s match against Portugal played during a tropical rainstorm, Da Rosa has never had his chance with the national team. He came through at Epif, one of Cape Verde’s most prestigious football academies, but struggled when he made the move to Portugal in 2015. After bouncing around the lower leagues there, he has now made Bulgaria his home with PFC Montana. With the emergence of CJ dos Santos, even once Vozinha retires after the World Cup, Da Rosa may find it hard to claim the No 1 shirt he has been heir to for nearly a decade.

data.name: Sidny Lopes Cabral
data.description: Three years ago, Cabral was playing in the fifth tier of German football. Three months ago he made his Champions League debut against Real Madrid. It has been quite a journey. Born in Rotterdam, at 23 Cabral has already spent time in the Netherlands, Sweden and Germany before breaking out as a star for the Portuguese side Estrela Amadora, from whom José Mourinho took him from to Benfica in December. While predominantly featuring as a left-back for Cape Verde, he is capable of playing every position on either wing and is completely two-footed. After scoring with both feet as part of a hat-trick this season, when asked which foot he prefers, he shrugged and said: “It doesn’t matter.”

data.name: Deroy Duarte
data.description: Deroy is the younger of the two but is the more successful. He was called up a full two years before Laros and has become a starter alongside Kevin Pina in the heart of the Blue Sharks’ midfield. A powerful holding midfielder, content to sit deep and let Pina and Jamiro Monteiro bomb forward, Duarte is one of the key cogs of Bubista’s side. But despite the younger brother being the better of the two, Deroy sees them as a package deal with the national team. "I actually haven't thought for a second about the possibility that we might not be selected," Deroy told ESPN after qualification. "Surely the national coach can't do that? Call up one of us and not the other?"

data.name: Laros Duarte
data.description: “He’s one of my best friends. Since we were little kids we dreamed of becoming professional footballers, and now we’ve achieved that and we’re facing each other. So it’s definitely special,” Laros Duarte told Sparta Rotterdam’s inhouse media when he faced off against his younger brother, Deroy, for the first time, for PSV Jong and Jong Sparta eight years ago. Now the midfielders are going to the World Cup together. Laros paved the way for his brother at Sparta Rotterdam’s academy, having joined as a five-year-old. The brothers are inseparable, perhaps best exemplified as the pair ended Cape Verde’s World Cup qualification against Eswatini together in the centre of the park.  "This has been a dream since childhood, to experience things like this together. A World Cup with Cape Verde, that is truly indescribable," Laros told the Dutch broadcaster Nos.

data.name: Yannick Semedo
data.description: "It's amazing,” says Leovigildo Ribeiro, the man who discovered Semedo as a child, of seeing him play for Cape Verde. “It was very emotional. I’m like a father to him.” Semedo, whose real father died when he was just a boy, was spotted at a youth game in his hometown, Cidade Velha, by Ribeiro, who brought the ball-winning midfielder to the capital to play for Celtic da Praia (Ribeiro’s family club, who were inspired after watching Jock Stein’s Lisbon Lions). Originally a centre-back, Semedo now plays as a holding midfielder, having enjoyed a career in the lower leagues of Portugal. Now Semedo could be lining up against Rodri, Pedri and co at the World Cup. "It's a dream,” says Ribeiro of seeing Semedo at a World Cup. “I'll be in the US to watch Semedo and the Selecao play."

data.name: Willy Semedo
data.description: Semedo chose the road less taken. Despite being born and raised in the suburbs of Paris, Semedo’s football opportunities were extremely limited. After failing to break through into professional football in France, he made the bold decision to move to Cyprus to play for the fourth division side Alki Oroklini. In four seasons with the club he earned three promotions. After stints around Europe and Saudi Arabia, the pacy winger landed back in Cyprus with Omonia Nicosia, whom he powered to a first title in four years. He is arguably Cape Verde’s most in-form player at a club level. He was the Cyprus Sports Writers’ player of the year in 2025 and this season became the first player to register double digits in goals and assists in the Cypriot first division’s history.

data.name: Telmo Arcanjo
data.description: One of Cape Verde’s long list of players whose family members also played for the Blue Sharks, Telmo Arcanjo followed in the footsteps of his older brother, Fábio. But unlike his brother, Telmo has become a key part of the Cape Verde squad despite suffering an anterior cruciate ligament tear in May 2023. He described the recovery period as “14 months of immense pain, sacrifice, and a longing to step on to the pitch again”. The Lisbon-born attacking midfielder helped Vitória de Guimarães to win the Taça da Liga this season, the club’s third ever piece of silverware. In Portugal Arcanjo is best known for a moment in October when as he was coming off the bench against Famalicão but had his phone in his hand. There was an explanation, though: he was talking to the head coach, Luís Pinto, who was in the stands having been sent off in their previous match.

data.name: Dailon Livramento
data.description: Some people are destined for greatness and Livramento was one of them. The son of the Cape Verdean musical legend Marizia do Rosario, Livramento made his singing debut as a one-year-old when his baby talk was featured in Marizia’s song Daily dedicated to him. The forward himself sings and his brother, Jerzy, is part of the Dutch hip-hop group Broederliefde. Despite being called up for the first time only in 2024, he already has seven goals for the Blue Sharks, four of them crucial in qualifying, including both the goals that sent Cape Verde to the World Cup against Cameroon and Eswatini. “When I scored I pointed to my wrist,” he told the Guardian after the match about his now famous celebration. “It’s time to go to the World Cup.” In Daily, Marizia describes Livramento as her “everything in the world”; little did she know that he would come to mean just as much to the rest of her tiny island homeland.

data.name: Ryan Mendes
data.description: The greatest of all time, Ryan Mendes is synonymous with the Blue Sharks. He is the nation’s captain, top scorer, most capped player and at the World Cup could become the first centurion in Cape Verdean history. The right-winger was an explosive talent, moving to the famous Le Havre academy where he came through alongside Paul Pogba, Benjamin Mendy and later Riyad Mahrez. It was actually Mendes who Leicester were looking at when theur chief scout, Steve Walsh, visited Le Havre in 2012. A member of Le Havre’s official supporters group told the BBC: “Ryan Mendes for sure was the better player. We expected him to reach a higher level than Riyad. Ryan was a fantastic player but he got a bad injury.” That ankle injury set back Mendes’ career at club level, but the 36-year-old has never let down his country and will captain the nation to their first World Cup.

data.name: Nuno da Costa
data.description: Another of Cape Verde’s late bloomers, Da Costa didn’t turn professional until he was 24. The versatile forward was studying at college while playing for Sporting Aubagne in the fifth tier of French football before signing his first professional contract with Valenciennes. While in France he was at the centre of a storm when the beIN Sports pundit and former French international Daniel Bravo, incredibly, described a Da Costa goal for Strasbourg as, “not bad for a black guy”. A year later, in 2020, the forward capable of playing anywhere across Cape Verde’s attack, signed for Nottingham Forest but left 10 months later. He later accused Forest of being a club that “move players around and replace them like objects”.

data.name: Steven Moreira
data.description: A former French youth international, for Steven Moreira the World Cup is a homecoming. Since signing with Columbus Crew in 2021 he has been one of the most consistent defenders in MLS. In 2023 he lifted the MLS Cup with the Crew and a season later, he was named defender of the year. Cape Verde’s starting right back will have to hope the Blue Sharks don’t come up against Scotland at the World Cup. The 31-year-old was spotted in the crowd when Rangers beat Celtic 3-1, leading to the sacking of Moreira’s former coach Wilfried Nancy. The defender became public enemy No 1 after the match when he defended Nancy, saying: “I really think that the MLS is way, way better than the Scottish League. I was very surprised how bad it was. That’s just my opinion.”

data.name: CJ dos Santos
data.description: Despite the number of Cape Verdeans living in the US exceeding the number living on the islands, the goalkeeper CJ Dos Santos is the only US-born player in the team and if he made his debut at the World Cup would become only the second American-born player to represent the Blue Sharks. Born in Philadelphia, the keeper spent time in Benfica’s academy before joining Inter Miami. Last year he moved to the new franchise San Diego FC, where he starred in the club’s record-setting first season in MLS. In the playoffs he fractured his nose, forcing him now to wear a face mask, earning him the nickname Batman. So excited by Dos Santos’s potential were the Blue Sharks that he was called up for their March friendlies before he had even been approved to switch nationality.

data.name: Wagner Pina
data.description: “Whoever bet on him as a right-sided player made a winning bet," Pina’s former youth coach Marcio Moreira told A Bola after the Lisbon-born player had been turned from a midfielder to a full-back. Moreira went on to boldly claim Pina would make a move to one of Portugal’s giants. But the now Turkish-based full-back had other ideas. Speaking to ZeroZero last year he said: "I want to reach the Premier League and play in an international competition for Cape Verde." With Pina heavily linked with a summer move to Fulham, he’s on his way to fulfilling both in the space of two months.

data.name: Kelvin Pires
data.description: Pires caused quite a stir in 2019 when he was called up for the Blue Sharks while still playing for Batuque FC in Cape Verde. No Cape Verdean had represented the national team while playing domestically for more than six years. A year later, the centre-back traded the beaches of São Vicente for the mountain valleys of Slovakia, where he spent four years before moving to Finland, where he now plays for Seinäjoen Jalkapallokerho. Coincidentally, Pires’s only goal for Cape Verde came against Finland in March, when the two sides met in the Fifa Series.

data.name: Hélio Varela
data.description: It’s not the first time Varela has been called up for an international tournament with Cape Verde, but he’ll be hoping this one goes differently than the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations. Having made his debut with the Blue Sharks just a few months before the Afcon, he was included in the squad but eventually refused the travel, deciding to stay with his club, Portimonense. The decision made Varela and the club public enemies with Cape Verde’s football federation president, Mario Semedo, who called the club and head coach, Paulo Sérgio, “petty” for pressuring Varela to not go to the Afcon. “They put enormous pressure on him not to come to the national team,” he said. Thankfully for the right-footed left winger, everything seems to have been forgiven.
